infobox.value: ([2020](/wiki/2020_US_census "2020 US census")) Detailed European responses only[[a]](#cite_note-1) 58.8% of the White alone population[[1]](#cite_note-2) **204.3M** [white](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") (one race)[[2]](#cite_note-3) **235.4M** White alone or [in combination](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans")  **96.5 million**[[b]](#cite_note-4) (white population who did not report a more detailed write-in response)[[3]](#cite_note-5) | |

are [Americans](/wiki/Americans "Americans") of [European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") ancestry.[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-MinahanEuropean-7) This term includes both people who descend from the first European settlers in the area of the present-day United States and people who descend from more recent European arrivals. Since the 17th century, European Americans have been the largest [panethnic group](/wiki/Panethnicity "Panethnicity") in what is now the United States. According to the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), 58.8% of the [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") alone population and 56.1% of the White alone or in combination gave a detailed European write-in response.[[6]](#cite_note-8)

| Predominantly [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_United_States "Christianity in the United States") (Mainly [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States"), [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States"), and [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_the_United_States "Eastern Orthodoxy in the United States")); Minority religions: [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_the_United_States "Judaism in the United States"), [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_States "Islam in the United States"), [Neo-Paganism](/wiki/Modern_paganism_in_the_United_States "Modern paganism in the United States"), [Irreligion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_States "Irreligion in the United States"), [Atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") | |

**European Americans** are [Americans](/wiki/Americans "Americans") of [European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") ancestry.[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-MinahanEuropean-7) This term includes both people who descend from the first European settlers in the area of the present-day United States and people who descend from more recent European arrivals. Since the 17th century, European Americans have been the largest [panethnic group](/wiki/Panethnicity "Panethnicity") in what is now the United States. According to the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), 58.8% of the [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") alone population and 56.1% of the White alone or in combination gave a detailed European write-in response.[[6]](#cite_note-8)

The [Spaniards](/wiki/Spanish_Americans "Spanish Americans") were the first Europeans to establish a continuous presence in what is now the [contiguous United States](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States"), although arriving in small numbers, with [Martín de Argüelles](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_de_Arg%C3%BCelles "Martín de Argüelles") (b. 1566) in [St. Augustine](/wiki/St._Augustine,_Florida "St. Augustine, Florida"), then a part of [Spanish Florida](/wiki/Spanish_Florida "Spanish Florida"),[[7]](#cite_note-loc-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10) and the [Russians](/wiki/Russian_Americans "Russian Americans") were the first Europeans to settle in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), establishing [Russian America](/wiki/Russian_America "Russian America"). The first [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") child born in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") was [Virginia Dare](/wiki/Virginia_Dare "Virginia Dare"), born August 18, 1587. She was born in [Roanoke Colony](/wiki/Roanoke_Colony "Roanoke Colony"), located in present-day [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"), which was the first attempt, made during the reign of [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I "Queen Elizabeth I"), to establish a permanent [English settlement](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions "English overseas possessions") in North America.

In the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), [British Americans](/wiki/British_Americans "British Americans") (46.6 million), [German Americans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") (45 million), [Irish Americans](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") (38.6 million), [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") (16.8 million) and [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") (8.6 million) were the five largest self-reported European ancestry groups in the United States.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

The 2020 census was the first census to allow data collection on subtypes of Europeans. During previous surveys, the number of people with British ancestry was considered to be significantly under-counted, as many people in that demographic tended to identify themselves simply as [Americans](/wiki/American_ancestry "American ancestry") (20,151,829 or 7.2%).[[10]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) A 2015 genetic study of 148,789 [European Americans](/wiki/European_American "European American") concluded that British ancestry was the most common European ancestry among white Americans, with this component ranging between 20% and 55% of the total population in all 50 states, showing its highest levels in the same states where "American" ancestry predominated on the census.[[14]](#cite_note-16) The same applies to the number Americans of [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Americans "Spanish Americans") ancestry, as most people in that demographic tend to identify themselves as [Hispanic and Latino Americans](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") (65,140,276 or 19.4%), especially since the vast majority of this group and their ancestors came to the U.S. from [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") countries rather than immigrating directly from Spain.[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18) Studies show that European genetic ancestry, mainly from Spain, is the largest component in Hispanic Americans, with a mean of 65.1% European genetic ancestry according to one study from 2014.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

An increasing number of people ignore the ancestry or origins question or chose no specific ancestral group such as "American or United States". In the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census") this represented over 56.1 million or 19.9% of the United States population, an increase from 26.2 million (10.5%) in 1990 and 38.2 million (16.9%) in 1980 and are specified as "unclassified" and "not reported".[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21) In the 2020 U.S. census, 96.58 million people did not report any detailed white ethnic origins and are "Not specified".[[20]](#cite_note-22)

### Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Use")]

In 1995, as part of a review of the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget")'s Statistical Policy Directive No. 15 (Race and Ethnic Standards for Federal Statistics and Administrative Reporting), a survey was conducted of census recipients to determine their preferred terminology for the racial/ethnic groups defined in the Directive. For the *White* group, *European American* came a distant third, preferred by only 2.35% of panel interviewees, as opposed to *White*, which was preferred by 61.66%.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

The term is sometimes used interchangeably with Caucasian American, [White American](/wiki/White_American "White American"), and [Anglo-American](/wiki/Anglo-America "Anglo-America") in the United States.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origin")]

In contexts such as medical research, terms such as "white" and "European" have been criticized for vagueness and blurring important distinctions between different groups that happen to fit within the label.[[23]](#cite_note-Bhopal-25) Margo Adair suggests that viewing Americans of European descent as a single group contributes to the "[wonder-breading](/wiki/Wonder_Bread "Wonder Bread")" of the United States, eradicating the cultural heritage of individual European ethnicities.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

### Subgroups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Subgroups")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/The_American_Museum_journal_%28c1900-%281918%29%29_%2818161635395%29.jpg/330px-The_American_Museum_journal_%28c1900-%281918%29%29_%2818161635395%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_American_Museum_journal_(c1900-(1918))_(18161635395).jpg)

Racial types of European Americans as published in "The American Museum Journal" between 1900 and 1918.

There are several subgroupings of European Americans.[[25]](#cite_note-27) While these categories may be approximately defined, often due to the imprecise or cultural regionalization of Europe, the subgroups are nevertheless used widely in cultural or ethnic identification.[[26]](#cite_note-28) This is particularly the case in diasporic populations, as with European people in the United States generally.[[27]](#cite_note-29) In alphabetical order, some of the subgroups are:

* **Northwestern European Americans**, including [Austrian Americans](/wiki/Austrian_Americans "Austrian Americans"), [Belgian Americans](/wiki/Belgian_Americans "Belgian Americans"), [British Americans](/wiki/British_Americans "British Americans") ([Cornish Americans](/wiki/Cornish_Americans "Cornish Americans"), [English Americans](/wiki/English_Americans "English Americans"), [Manx Americans](/wiki/Manx_Americans "Manx Americans"), [Scotch-Irish Americans](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_Americans "Scotch-Irish Americans"), [Scottish Americans](/wiki/Scottish_Americans "Scottish Americans"), [Welsh Americans](/wiki/Welsh_Americans "Welsh Americans")), [Dutch Americans](/wiki/Dutch_Americans "Dutch Americans"), [French Americans](/wiki/French_Americans "French Americans") ([Breton Americans](/wiki/Breton_Americans "Breton Americans")), [German Americans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), [Irish Americans](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), [Luxembourger Americans](/wiki/Luxembourger_Americans "Luxembourger Americans"), [Nordic and Scandinavian Americans](/wiki/Nordic_and_Scandinavian_Americans "Nordic and Scandinavian Americans") ([Danish Americans](/wiki/Danish_Americans "Danish Americans"), [Finnish Americans](/wiki/Finnish_Americans "Finnish Americans"), [Icelandic Americans](/wiki/Icelandic_Americans "Icelandic Americans"), [Norwegian Americans](/wiki/Norwegian_Americans "Norwegian Americans"), [Swedish Americans](/wiki/Swedish_Americans "Swedish Americans")), and [Swiss Americans](/wiki/Swiss_Americans "Swiss Americans"), or "Old Immigrants" (the first waves of which arrived pre-1881)
* **Eastern European Americans**, including [Belarusian Americans](/wiki/Belarusian_Americans "Belarusian Americans"), [Czech Americans](/wiki/Czech_Americans "Czech Americans"), [Estonian Americans](/wiki/Estonian_Americans "Estonian Americans"), [Hungarian Americans](/wiki/Hungarian_Americans "Hungarian Americans"), [Latvian Americans](/wiki/Latvian_Americans "Latvian Americans"), [Lithuanian Americans](/wiki/Lithuanian_Americans "Lithuanian Americans"), [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans"), [Russian Americans](/wiki/Russian_Americans "Russian Americans"), [Slovak Americans](/wiki/Slovak_Americans "Slovak Americans"), and [Ukrainian Americans](/wiki/Ukrainian_Americans "Ukrainian Americans"), or "New Immigrants" (the first large waves of which arrived 1881–1965)
* **Southern European Americans**, including [Albanian Americans](/wiki/Albanian_Americans "Albanian Americans"), [Bosnian Americans](/wiki/Bosnian_Americans "Bosnian Americans"), [Bulgarian Americans](/wiki/Bulgarian_Americans "Bulgarian Americans"), [Croatian Americans](/wiki/Croatian_Americans "Croatian Americans"), [Cypriot Americans](/wiki/Cypriot_Americans "Cypriot Americans"), [Greek Americans](/wiki/Greek_Americans "Greek Americans"), [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans"), [Maltese Americans](/wiki/Maltese_Americans "Maltese Americans"), [Macedonian Americans](/wiki/Macedonian_Americans "Macedonian Americans"), [Moldovan Americans](/wiki/Moldovan_Americans "Moldovan Americans"), [Montenegrin Americans](/wiki/Montenegrin_Americans "Montenegrin Americans"), [Portuguese Americans](/wiki/Portuguese_Americans "Portuguese Americans"), [Romanian Americans](/wiki/Romanian_Americans "Romanian Americans"), [Serbian Americans](/wiki/Serbian_Americans "Serbian Americans"), [Slovenian Americans](/wiki/Slovenian_Americans "Slovenian Americans"), and [Spanish Americans](/wiki/Spanish_Americans "Spanish Americans") ([Basque Americans](/wiki/Basque_Americans "Basque Americans"), [Catalan Americans](/wiki/Catalan_Americans "Catalan Americans"), [Galician Americans](/wiki/Galician_Americans "Galician Americans")), also "New Immigrants" or "New Immigrants"(the first large waves of which arrived 1881–1965)

Before the arrival of Europeans, [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") predominantly inhabited the United States. The earliest Europeans to colonize North America were the small number of Spaniards. The first Spanish colonization was in 1565 at [St. Augustine, Florida](/wiki/St._Augustine,_Florida "St. Augustine, Florida").[[30]](#cite_note-35) One of the most significant Spanish explorers was [Hernando De Soto](/wiki/Hernando_De_Soto "Hernando De Soto"), a conquistador who accompanied [Francisco Pizzaro](/wiki/Francisco_Pizzaro "Francisco Pizzaro") during his [conquest of the Inca Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Inca_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire").

Leaving Havana, Cuba, in 1539, De Soto's expedition landed in Florida. It explored the southeastern area of the United States. They reached as far as the Mississippi River in search of riches and fortune. Another Spaniard who explored the United States, [Francisco Vázquez de Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_V%C3%A1zquez_de_Coronado "Francisco Vázquez de Coronado"), set out from [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") in 1540 in search of the mythical [Seven Cities of Gold](/wiki/Seven_Cities_of_Gold "Seven Cities of Gold"). Coronado's expedition traveled to Kansas and the Grand Canyon but failed to discover gold or treasure. However, Coronado left a gift of horses to the Plains Indians. Italian explorer [Giovanni da Verrazano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazano "Giovanni da Verrazano") and Frenchman [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") are other Europeans who explored the United States. The Spaniards viewed the French as threatening their trade route along the Gulf Stream.[[31]](#cite_note-36)

Since 1607, some 57 million immigrants from other lands have come to the United States. Approximately 10 million passed through on their way to some other place or returned to their homelands, leaving a net gain of 47 million people.[[32]](#cite_note-37)

During the 1600s and 1700s, Europeans arrived in North America in search of religious freedom, economic prospects, and political independence.[[33]](#cite_note-38)

### Shifts in European migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Shifts in European migration")]

Before 1881, the vast majority of immigrants, almost 86% of the total, arrived from [Northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe"), principally [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), known as "Old Immigration". Between 1881 and 1893, the pattern shifted in the sources of U.S. "New Immigration." Between 1894 and 1914, immigrants from [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), and [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") accounted for 69% of the total.[[34]](#cite_note-39)[[35]](#cite_note-40)[[36]](#cite_note-SzucsLuebking2006-41) Prior to 1960, the overwhelming majority came from Europe or of [European descent](/wiki/European_Canadians "European Canadians") from Canada. Immigration from Europe as a proportion of new arrivals has declined since the mid-20th century, with 75.0% of the total foreign-born population born in Europe compared to 12.1% recorded in the 2010 census.[[37]](#cite_note-:0-42)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture")]

Further information: [Culture of the United States](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_States "Culture of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Motherhood_and_apple_pie.jpg/330px-Motherhood_and_apple_pie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motherhood_and_apple_pie.jpg)

American cultural icons, [apple pie](/wiki/Apple_pie "Apple pie"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"), and the [American flag](/wiki/American_flag "American flag"). All have European influence, primarily from the British.

As the largest component of the American population, the overall American culture deeply reflects the European-influenced culture that predates the United States of America as an independent state. Much of American culture shows influences from the diverse nations of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), such as the [English](/wiki/English_Americans "English Americans"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_Americans "Cornish Americans"), [Manx](/wiki/Manx_Americans "Manx Americans"), [Scotch-Irish](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_Americans "Scotch-Irish Americans"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Americans "Scottish Americans") and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_Americans "Welsh Americans"). Colonial ties to the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") spread the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), legal system and other cultural attributes.[[5]](#cite_note-MinahanEuropean-7)

Scholar [David Hackett Fischer](/wiki/David_Hackett_Fischer "David Hackett Fischer") asserts in *[Albion's Seed: Four British Folkways in America](/wiki/Albion%27s_Seed:_Four_British_Folkways_in_America "Albion's Seed: Four British Folkways in America")* that the folkways of four groups of people who moved from distinct regions of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") persisted and provide a substantial cultural basis for much of the modern [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[58]](#cite_note-70) Fischer explains "the origins and stability of a social system which for two centuries has remained stubbornly [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") in its politics, [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") in its economy, [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") in its laws and [individualist](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism") in its society and [pluralistic](/wiki/Cultural_pluralism "Cultural pluralism") in its culture."[[59]](#cite_note-71)

Much of the European-American cultural lineage can be traced back to [Western](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe"), which is institutionalized in the government, traditions, and civic education in the United States.[[60]](#cite_note-Kirk-72)
Since most later European Americans have assimilated into American culture, many Americans of European ancestry now generally express their personal ethnic ties sporadically and symbolically and do not consider their specific ethnic origins to be essential to their identity; however, European American ethnic expression has been revived since the 1960s.[[61]](#cite_note-Randolph-73) Some European Americans such as [Italians](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), [Greeks](/wiki/Greek_Americans "Greek Americans"), [Poles](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans"), [Germans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainian_Americans "Ukrainian Americans"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), and others have maintained high levels of ethnic identity. In the 1960s, the melting pot ideal to some extent gave way to increased interest in cultural pluralism, strengthening affirmations of ethnic identity among various American ethnic groups, European as well as others.[[61]](#cite_note-Randolph-73)

### Law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Law")]

The [American legal system](/wiki/Law_of_the_United_States "Law of the United States") also has its roots in [French](/wiki/France "France") philosophy with the separation of powers and the federal system[[62]](#cite_note-74) along with [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law") in common law.[[63]](#cite_note-75)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Cuisine of the United States](/wiki/Cuisine_of_the_United_States "Cuisine of the United States")

See also: [Italian-American cuisine](/wiki/Italian-American_cuisine "Italian-American cuisine") and [Greek-American cuisine](/wiki/Greek-American_cuisine "Greek-American cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Hamburger_%28black_bg%29.jpg/250px-Hamburger_%28black_bg%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hamburger_(black_bg).jpg)

Hamburgers were invented by German immigrants.

* **[Apple pie](/wiki/Apple_pie "Apple pie")** – [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") was the first region to experience large-scale [English colonization](/wiki/English_colonial_empire "English colonial empire") in the early 17th century, beginning in 1620, and it was dominated by [East Anglian](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") Calvinists, better known as the [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan"). Baking was a particular favorite of the New Englanders and was the origin of dishes seen today as quintessentially "American", such as [apple pie](/wiki/Apple_pie "Apple pie") and the oven-roasted [Thanksgiving](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)") [turkey](/wiki/Turkey_(bird) "Turkey (bird)"), a bird that although not found in Europe has become linked in tradition and symbolism to the early European immigrants.[[64]](#cite_note-76) "As American as apple pie" is a well-known phrase used to suggest that something is all-American.
* **[Hamburger](/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger")** – Although the origins of the hamburger, including the country in which it was first served, are subjects of debate, the hamburger first became widely marketed in the United States[[65]](#cite_note-77) and has been internationally known for decades as a symbol of American [fast food](/wiki/Fast_food "Fast food").
* **[Buffalo wings](/wiki/Buffalo_wings "Buffalo wings")** – Invented in 1964 at [Anchor Bar](/wiki/Anchor_Bar "Anchor Bar") in [Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") by Italian-American Teressa Bellissimo. Now popular all over the country, it has become a symbol of American cuisine.[[66]](#cite_note-78)
* **[Hot dog](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog")** – Hot dogs were brought to New York by German immigrants.[[67]](#cite_note-79)
* **[Pizza](/wiki/Pizza "Pizza")** – Italian immigrants from [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") brought pizza to the United States.[[68]](#cite_note-80)
* **[Fried chicken](/wiki/Fried_chicken "Fried chicken")** – Scottish immigrants brought fried chicken to the Southern United States. Enslaved African Americans began cooking fried chicken based on the recipes from white Scottish slaveholders.[[69]](#cite_note-81)

### Thanksgiving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Thanksgiving")]

* **[Thanksgiving](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)")** – In the United States, it has become a national secular holiday (official since [1863](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)")) with religious origins. The first Thanksgiving was celebrated by [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") settlers to give thanks to God and the [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") for helping the [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)") of [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony") survive the brutal winter.[[70]](#cite_note-82) The modern Thanksgiving holiday traces its origins from a 1621 celebration at the Plymouth Plantation, where the Plymouth settlers held a harvest feast with the [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") after a successful growing season. [William Bradford](/wiki/William_Bradford_(Plymouth_governor) "William Bradford (Plymouth governor)") is credited as the first to proclaim the American cultural event which is generally referred to as the "[First Thanksgiving](/wiki/Thanksgiving#The_"First"_Thanksgiving? "Thanksgiving")".

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sports")]

See also: [Origins of baseball](/wiki/Origins_of_baseball "Origins of baseball")

* **[Baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball")** – The earliest recorded game of base-ball involved the family of the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"), played indoors in London in November 1748. The Prince is reported as playing "Bass-Ball" again in September 1749 in Walton-on-Thames, Surrey, against Lord Middlesex.[[71]](#cite_note-83) English lawyer William Bray recorded a game of baseball on [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday") 1755 in [Guildford](/wiki/Guildford "Guildford"), [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey"); Bray's diary was verified as authentic in September 2008.[[72]](#cite_note-84)[[73]](#cite_note-85) This early form of the game was apparently brought to North America by English immigrants. The first appearance of the term that exists in print was in "[A Little Pretty Pocket-Book](/wiki/A_Little_Pretty_Pocket-Book "A Little Pretty Pocket-Book")" in 1744, where it is called Base-Ball.
* **[American football](/wiki/American_football "American football")** – can be traced to modified early versions of [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") played in [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") mixed with and ultimately changed by [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") innovations which led over time to the finished version of the game from 1876 to now. The basic set of rules was first developed in American universities in the mid-19th century.[[74]](#cite_note-86)
* **[Golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf")** - Golf originated from [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in the 15th century, the first course in Scotland being [St Andrews](/wiki/St_Andrews_Links "St Andrews Links"). The first golf course in America was founded by a [Scot](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") [John Reid](/wiki/John_Reid_(businessman) "John Reid (businessman)") in 1888, and was named after the first Scottish golf club [Saint Andrew's Golf Club](/wiki/Saint_Andrew%27s_Golf_Club_(New_York) "Saint Andrew's Golf Club (New York)") located in [Yonkers, New York](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"), from here golf soared as a national hobby, and by the turn of the 20th Century there was more than 1,000 golf courses in North America.[[75]](#cite_note-87)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Music")]

Another area of cultural influence are [American Patriotic songs](/wiki/American_patriotic_music "American patriotic music"):

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/John_Philip_Sousa_in_1922.jpg/250px-John_Philip_Sousa_in_1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Philip_Sousa_in_1922.jpg)

[John Philip Sousa](/wiki/John_Philip_Sousa "John Philip Sousa") composed  [The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post_(march) "The Washington Post (march)"), a patriotic American march.

* **[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")** – takes its melody from the 18th-century English song "[To Anacreon in Heaven](/wiki/To_Anacreon_in_Heaven "To Anacreon in Heaven")" written by [John Stafford Smith](/wiki/John_Stafford_Smith "John Stafford Smith") for the [Anacreontic Society](/wiki/Anacreontic_Society "Anacreontic Society"), a men's social club in London and lyrics written by American [Francis Scott Key](/wiki/Francis_Scott_Key "Francis Scott Key"). This became a well-known and recognized patriotic song throughout the United States, which was officially designated as the American national anthem in 1931.[[76]](#cite_note-88)[[77]](#cite_note-89)[[78]](#cite_note-90)

Before 1931, other songs served as the hymns of American officialdom.

* **[Amazing Grace](/wiki/Amazing_Grace "Amazing Grace")** – written by British poet and [clergyman](/wiki/Clergyman "Clergyman") [John Newton](/wiki/John_Newton "John Newton"). Popular among [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), it became an icon in American culture and has been used for a variety of secular purposes and marketing campaigns.[[79]](#cite_note-91)
* **[Hail, Columbia](/wiki/Hail,_Columbia "Hail, Columbia")** – initial presidential inauguration song up until early 20th century. Now used for the [Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States").
* **[Battle Hymn of the Republic](/wiki/Battle_Hymn_of_the_Republic "Battle Hymn of the Republic")** – Patriotic song sung during the civil war time between 1861 and 1865.

## Admixture in Whites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Americans&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Admixture in Whites")]

Some European Americans have varying amounts of [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") and Native [African](/wiki/Demographics_of_Africa "Demographics of Africa") ancestry. From the [23andMe](/wiki/23andMe "23andMe") database, about 5 to at least 13 percent of self-identified European American Southerners have greater than 1 percent native [African](/wiki/Demographics_of_Africa "Demographics of Africa") ancestry.[[80]](#cite_note-23andme-92) [Southern states](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") with the highest African American populations tended to have the highest percentages of hidden African ancestry.[[81]](#cite_note-Hadly-93) European Americans on average are: "98.6 percent Native European, 0.19 percent Native African and 0.18 percent Native American." Inferred British/Irish ancestry is found in European Americans from all states at mean proportions of
above 20%, and represents a majority of ancestry, above 50% mean proportion, in states such
as Mississippi, Arkansas, and Tennessee. Scandinavian
ancestry in European Americans is highly localized; most states show only trace mean
proportions of Scandinavian ancestry, while it comprises a significant proportion, upwards of
10%, of ancestry in European Americans from Minnesota and the Dakotas.[[80]](#cite_note-23andme-92)[[81]](#cite_note-Hadly-93)
